narrative_blocks.detail: Merrill Edwards Gates was inaugurated President of Rutgers College on June 20, 1882. During his presidency he built Winants Hall (Rutgers' first dormitory, completed 1890, named for Garrett E. Winants) and New Jersey Hall for chemistry and biology instruction. He established the Agricultural Experiment Station under the Hatch Act (1887) and expanded funding for the scientific school under the Morrill Act (1890). He resigned in 1890 to become President of Amherst College, serving 1890-1899. Per the Rutgers biography: "His administration was marked by a large increase in the Faculty and endowments of the College. The curriculum was extended and the library greatly enlarged."
narrative_blocks.body: Led two of America's most distinguished universities; built Rutgers' first dormitory (Winants Hall) and the State Laboratory.

narrative_blocks.detail: After leaving Amherst, Gates served as Chairman of the U.S. Board of Indian Commissioners (1889-1899) and then Secretary (1899-1912) — 23 years total shaping federal Indian policy during the assimilation era. He chaired the Lake Mohonk Conferences on the Indian Question. His published works include Land and Law as Agents in Educating the Indians (1885). His work is viewed today through a complex lens: well-intentioned by the standards of his era, but deeply paternalistic and assimilationist.
narrative_blocks.body: Chairman then Secretary of the U.S. Board of Indian Commissioners; wrote on land, law, and Indian education.

narrative_blocks.header: Albany Academy principal — enrollment from 70 to 800
narrative_blocks.detail: Before his university presidencies, Gates served as principal of The Albany Academy for 12 years, extending its curriculum from 8 to 12 years and growing enrollment from 70 to 800 students. In 1875 he was elected Chancellor of the University of Tennessee but declined, remaining at Albany. He traveled abroad twice (1872 visiting Rugby and Oxford; 1879-80 to France, Italy, Egypt, Palestine, and Greece).
narrative_blocks.body: Grew Albany Academy from 70 to 800 students over 12 years; declined the Chancellorship of the University of Tennessee.

narrative_blocks.header: Descended from Jonathan Edwards — and the Underground Railroad
narrative_blocks.detail: The Rutgers College biography explicitly notes: "Through his mother he is descended from Jonathan Edwards" — Fanny Jennette Parsons (HD1673) was the great-great-granddaughter of Rev. Jonathan Edwards and Sarah Pierpont, and a great-great-great-grandchild of Rev. Thomas Hooker. His father Seth M. Gates was the antislavery U.S. Congressman whose Warsaw NY home was a station on the Underground Railroad for 15+ years. A Southern planter offered $500 for his father's apprehension.
narrative_blocks.body: Through his mother Fanny Parsons, a direct descendant of Rev. Jonathan Edwards (gen 4 Hooker line); his father Seth Gates ran an Underground Railroad station.

narrative_blocks.detail: Merrill Edwards Gates was inaugurated President of Rutgers College on June 20, 1882. During his presidency he built Winants Hall (Rutgers' first dormitory, completed 1890, named for Garrett E. Winants) and New Jersey Hall for chemistry and biology instruction. He established the Agricultural Experiment Station under the Hatch Act (1887) and expanded funding for the scientific school under the Morrill Act (1890). He resigned in 1890 to become President of Amherst College, serving 1890-1899. Per the Rutgers biography: "His administration was marked by a large increase in the Faculty and endowments of the College. The curriculum was extended and the library greatly enlarged."
narrative_blocks.body: Led two of America's most distinguished universities; built Rutgers' first dormitory (Winants Hall) and the State Laboratory.

narrative_blocks.detail: After leaving Amherst, Gates served as Chairman of the U.S. Board of Indian Commissioners (1889-1899) and then Secretary (1899-1912) — 23 years total shaping federal Indian policy during the assimilation era. He chaired the Lake Mohonk Conferences on the Indian Question. His published works include Land and Law as Agents in Educating the Indians (1885). His work is viewed today through a complex lens: well-intentioned by the standards of his era, but deeply paternalistic and assimilationist.
narrative_blocks.body: Chairman then Secretary of the U.S. Board of Indian Commissioners; wrote on land, law, and Indian education.

narrative_blocks.header: Albany Academy principal — enrollment from 70 to 800
narrative_blocks.detail: Before his university presidencies, Gates served as principal of The Albany Academy for 12 years, extending its curriculum from 8 to 12 years and growing enrollment from 70 to 800 students. In 1875 he was elected Chancellor of the University of Tennessee but declined, remaining at Albany. He traveled abroad twice (1872 visiting Rugby and Oxford; 1879-80 to France, Italy, Egypt, Palestine, and Greece).
narrative_blocks.body: Grew Albany Academy from 70 to 800 students over 12 years; declined the Chancellorship of the University of Tennessee.

narrative_blocks.header: Descended from Jonathan Edwards — and the Underground Railroad
narrative_blocks.detail: The Rutgers College biography explicitly notes: "Through his mother he is descended from Jonathan Edwards" — Fanny Jennette Parsons (HD1673) was the great-great-granddaughter of Rev. Jonathan Edwards and Sarah Pierpont, and a great-great-great-grandchild of Rev. Thomas Hooker. His father Seth M. Gates was the antislavery U.S. Congressman whose Warsaw NY home was a station on the Underground Railroad for 15+ years. A Southern planter offered $500 for his father's apprehension.
narrative_blocks.body: Through his mother Fanny Parsons, a direct descendant of Rev. Jonathan Edwards (gen 4 Hooker line); his father Seth Gates ran an Underground Railroad station.

narrative_blocks.header: A congressman with a price on his head
narrative_blocks.body: Gates served two terms in Congress as an antislavery Whig from New York, drafting the party's protest against the annexation of Texas. His stand so angered the South that a planter offered five hundred dollars for his capture.

narrative_blocks.header: His house hid fugitives in cellar and attic
narrative_blocks.body: Gates ran a station on the Underground Railroad from his Warsaw, New York, home, concealing freedom-seekers in its cellar and attic. The house still stands and is listed on the National Register of Historic Places.

narrative_blocks.header: He twice ran for lieutenant governor and lost
narrative_blocks.body: After Congress, Gates twice sought the lieutenant governorship of New York on antislavery tickets, as a Free Soiler in 1848 and a Free Democrat in 1852. Both campaigns failed, but they kept him at the center of the cause.

notable.notable_blurb: Antislavery congressman with a bounty on him

narrative_blocks.header: Cast the first iron ploughs in Canada
narrative_blocks.detail: Son of Eliphalet Parsons (H00904) and Martha Young; born 1789; baptized 1791 at Stockbridge MA. Moved through Broome County NY and Wilkes-Barre PA before relocating to Canada in 1824. Married Jennett Hepburn 1815. Two children: Lewis Eliphalet Parsons (HD1672, later provisional Governor of Alabama) and Fanny Jennette Parsons (HD1673, married Underground Railroad operator Seth Gates). Died September 2, 1827 at Chippewa (Chippawa), Ontario age 38. (Hinsdale family history; FindAGrave; WikiTree)
narrative_blocks.body: Erastus Parsons moved from Broome County NY to Upper Canada in 1824 and cast the first iron ploughs made in Canada before dying at 38.

INST017.name: Rutgers College (now Rutgers University)
INST017.notes: Founded 1766 as Queen's College. Renamed Rutgers College 1825. Now Rutgers, The State University of New Jersey. Merrill Edwards Gates (HD1687) served as 9th President 1882-1890. During his tenure: built Winants Hall (first dormitory), New Jersey Hall, established Agricultural Experiment Station (Hatch Act 1887), expanded curriculum and library. Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rutgers_University

INST018.name: Amherst College
INST018.notes: Founded 1821. One of the oldest liberal arts colleges in the United States. Merrill Edwards Gates (HD1687) served as 6th President 1890-1899. Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amherst_College David Peck Todd (HD1758) was astronomy professor and observatory director 1881-1917. Millicent Todd Bingham (HD1759) received honorary LL.D. 1957.

INST019.name: Albany Academy
INST019.notes: One of the oldest secondary schools in the United States. Merrill Edwards Gates (HD1687) served as principal for 12 years (c.1870-1882), growing enrollment from 70 to 800 students and extending the curriculum from 8 to 12 years. He declined the Chancellorship of the University of Tennessee (1875) to remain at Albany. Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Albany_Academy

INST020.name: University of Rochester
INST020.notes: Founded 1850. Merrill Edwards Gates (HD1687) graduated 1870 with honors in Mathematics, Latin, Greek, and the English Essay Prize. The university awarded him an honorary LL.D. in 1882. Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Rochester Asahel Clark Kendrick (I00943) was among the founding faculty (1850), served as inaugural Munro Professor of Greek 1850-1888, first librarian 1850-1866, acting president 1863 and 1877-78. His son Ryland Morris Kendrick (HD4668, class of 1889) taught Greek and Latin on the same faculty. Ryland's son Nathaniel Cooper Kendrick (HD4670) earned B.A. 1921 here before his Harvard doctorate. Nathaniel's daughter Ann Kendrick McNabb (HD4672) also received her B.A. in History here. Kendrick Hall, the first men's dormitory (1913), was named for Asahel.

INST021.name: U.S. Board of Indian Commissioners

sources.note: Merrill Edwards Gates; Board of Indian Commissioners

INST021.notes: Advisory body established 1869 to oversee federal Indian affairs and disbursement of funds. Abolished 1933. Merrill Edwards Gates (HD1687) served as Chairman 1889-1899 and Secretary 1899-1912 — 23 years total shaping federal Indian policy. He also chaired the Lake Mohonk Conferences on the Indian Question.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.

INST144.name: American Philosophical Society
INST144.notes:
Founded 1743 Philadelphia by Benjamin Franklin. The oldest learned society in the United States, modeled on the Royal Society of London. Membership election is one of the highest academic honors in American intellectual life — typically limited to roughly 1,000 living members worldwide.

HOOKER-TREE CONNECTIONS — 7 elected members across the colonial and 19th-century period:

EARLY REPUBLIC ERA:
- HD0346 President Ezra Stiles (1727-1795, Yale president, elected during early APS expansion)
- T01807 Captain Andrew Talcott (1797-1883, military engineer, Mexican War + Mississippi River surveys)

ANTEBELLUM ERA:
- TD0239 Judge Joel Jones (1795-1860, elected 1848 documented — Philadelphia jurist + Mayor of Philadelphia 1849-1851)

VICTORIAN ERA YALE CLUSTER:
- HD1973 Theodore Dwight Woolsey (1801-1889, Yale president 1846-1871)
- X00898 Edward Salisbury Dana (1849-1935, Yale mineralogist)
- HD4112 Thomas Burr Osborne (1859-1929, Yale agricultural chemist — discovered proteins in seeds)

GILDED AGE:
- HD1687 Merrill Edwards Gates (1848-1922, Rutgers + Amherst president)

Joel Jones's 1848 election as APS member coincided with his service as Mayor of Philadelphia — putting him at the center of the city's mid-19th-century intellectual life alongside the broader Philadelphia learned-society world (Franklin Institute, Athenaeum, etc.).
